core_behavior.note: Model A specifies a readers-writer lock with FIFO queue and write-preferring policy; Models B and C describe general concurrency management without committing to a specific mechanism, leaving the concurrency strategy unspecified per the critical behaviors list.

inputs.note: Model A defines typed inputs including timeout, priority, and requester identity; Model B uses HTTP request metadata; Model C describes a stream of requests with resource identifier and operation type, with no timeout or priority fields.

outputs.note: Model A returns structured status codes, version tokens, wait duration, and queue position; Model B returns responses and system logs; Model C returns operation-specific confirmations and error messages without version tokens or wait metrics.

assumptions.note: Model A assumes distributed lock manager, readers-writer lock, and idempotent writes; Model B assumes mutex/semaphore plus FIFO queue and connection pooling; Model C leaves the locking type open (optimistic or pessimistic) and mentions optional queue use, reflecting the unspecified concurrency mechanism.

failure_modes.note: Model A specifies only lock acquisition timeout with queue removal; Model B covers timeout, resource contention, system overload with load shedding, and generic errors; Model C covers conflict errors, deadlock detection and resolution, and resource unavailability.

edge_cases.note: Model A has an empty edge_cases array; Models B and C each populate edge cases including request cancellation, priority handling, and simultaneous read/write scenarios.

security_constraints.note: Model A has an empty security_constraints array; Model B specifies authentication, authorization, encryption, and RBAC; Model C specifies authentication, authorization, and audit logging.

performance_constraints.note: Model A has an empty performance_constraints array; Model B specifies concrete thresholds (< 500ms response, 100 concurrent requests/second); Model C specifies only qualitative scalability and latency goals without numeric thresholds.

summary: ASM (Assumption Divergence) appeared most frequently, occurring in 3 of the 8 fields, reflecting that the requirement left the concurrency mechanism, input schema, and output schema unspecified, leading models to independently introduce different design choices.
